measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
measure_name: Measure 1 - Strengthen communication on renovation obligations and opportunities
description:
Faced with the challenges of legibility, fair access to information and
mobilisation of all renovation stakeholders in Wallonia, the measure aims to set
up a centralised communication dedicated to information and activation for
renovation. This unified communication must centralise content, make users’
journeys clear and consistent, and support the action of field relays. It targets
both the residential sector and the non-residential, private and public sector
(municipalities, CPAS, etc.), as well as professional actors in renovation.
This measure constitutes a transversal axis of the Renovation Plan, at the service
of the legibility of legislative and organizational reforms. Its objective is to
create a unified, proactive and continuous communication strategy, enabling
both:
•
Strengthen understanding of obligations;
•
encourage mobilisation towards support schemes;
•
Encourage changes in behaviour related to the energy use of housing.
Specifically, the measure provides for:
•
Harmonisation and updating of information on energy renovation in
Wallonia (financial support, support, EPB standards) on a unified
portal, interoperable with the sites of the SPW and the other
administrations concerned;
•
Providing accessibledecision support tools: decision tree pointing to
the right device, global housing cost simulator, plain language content;
•
Activation of key moments in the life of the building (purchase,
inheritance, rental) by means of communication kits and a proactive
strategy for professional actors (architects, contractors, etc.).
The strategy will combine the direct dissemination of information and its
increased visibility in the spaces frequented by the target audiences.
Finally, continuous campaigns will highlight the co-benefits of renovation
(comfort, health, heritage value, economies, climate), supported by credible
feedback to stimulate demand.
quantified_objectives:
- ≥ 50 % awareness of the device in 2029
- ≥ 200 000 visits/month to the portal.
- 100% of regional sites related to energy renovation
harmonised (financial support, accompaniments, EPB standards) in
2028
budget:
- Single portal and digital tools: €€€€
- Media campaigns and content: €€€
- Kits & animation of relays: €€ €€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, one-stop shops, SWCS ACER actors

measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
measure_name: Measure 2 - Consolidate a centralised database on the energy performance of buildings
description:
Precise knowledge of the building stock is the condition for effectively steering
the renovation policy and ensuring compliance with the obligations set by the
European Directive EPB 2024/1275. However, in Wallonia, information on
buildings is now scattered among several registers (cadastre, EPB, audits,
permits, financial aid, municipal data), without interconnection or consolidated
vision. This fragmentation makes it impossible to reliably assess the state of
the building, the level of energy performance achieved and the monitoring of
renovations.
The consolidation of a centralised database addresses this strategic challenge.
This database will be linked to the building passport. It will make it possible to
identify all non-residential dwellings and buildings, to associate each of them
with a unique identifier and to link all available administrative, technical and
energy information to it. The automatic cross-breeding between EPB
certificates and rightholders in rem will ensure the traceability of the park and
will form the basis for the implementation and control of the renovation
obligations. This database will become the unique repository of the Walloon
building stock. It will prioritise the integration of EPB certificates, audits and
HVAC inspection reports.
The tool will enable the Region to ensure:
1. A comprehensive and dynamic view of the state of Walloon buildings
2. Automatic identification of uncertified or non-compliant units
3. Monitoring compliance with obligations and renovation trajectories
4. And a harmonised transmission of data to the European Commission
By ensuring consistency, security and continuous updating of data, this
database will be a key lever for energy planning, administrative transparency
and public policy impact assessment. Ultimately, it will allow Wallonia to have
a complete and dynamic energy mapping of buildings, a prerequisite for
achieving the decarbonisation of the park by 2050.
quantified_objectives:
- Implementation of a centralised database in 2028
- Set up automatic tracking of non-EPB units and their rightholders in
rem by 2032.
- Notify 100% of affected homeowners at least 3 years before the
renovation obligation deadlines.
budget:
- Technical design, interoperability and security €€€
- Management cost €€€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, Walloon Government

measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
measure_name: Measure 3 - Establish an observatory for renovation and building trades
description:
Wallonia has many sources of data on renovation and employment in the
building sector, but these are fragmented and incompletely exploited. While
the centralised database on the energy performance of buildings will provide
the technical basis for collection and harmonisation, there is currently a lack of
a tool capable of transforming this information into reliable indicators,
forward-looking analyses and strategic steering tools.
The EPBD 2024/1275 requires structured monitoring: renovation rate and
depth, actual performance, availability of skilled labour. The accumulated
delays in the objectification of renovations and occupations create a risk of
European non-compliance and reduce the credibility of Walloon policies. In
addition, the actors in the sector (companies, federations, training bodies) lack
visibility on future needs, which complicates planning.
The observatory will have a dual role:
1. Renovation: use data from the centralised database, permits, financial
support, audits, to measure the pace and depth of renovations, verify
the actual efficiency (before/after works) and monitor the impact of
regional policies.
2. On the trades: collect and cross-reference data from Statbel,
FOREM, IFAPME, Embuild, in order to identify critical jobs,
anticipate needs, adjust training policies and support sectors.
3. Materials: using the data from fiche measure 16 to monitor the
development of local and circular sectors (share of local materials,
available EPDs, rates of circular public procurement, technological
maturity).
The observatory will also be an instrument of transparency: a public scoreboard
will present key indicators to citizens, municipalities and businesses, while
more detailed reports will guide policy-makers.
quantified_objectives:
- Observatory operational in 2029, first report published in 2030
- Produce an indicator of the rate of deep renovation and the average
label of the park as early as 2028
- Tracking critical workforce and business needs
- Cover 80% of relevant administrative data in the analysis by 2030.
budget:
- Technical set-up of the observatory: €€
- Annual operation (analyses, publications, surveys): €
- Prospective studies (jobs, industrial sectors): €
- Communication and public scoreboard: €
stakeholders:
SPW, IWEPS, CEHD, FOREM and IFAPME, Embuild, Statbel, GRD,
communes and intercommunales

measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
measure_name: Measure 4 - Simplification of administrative procedures
description:
Administrative procedures are now one of the main obstacles to energy
renovation. Planning permit procedures, designed for construction projects, are
often disproportionate for simple renovation works. Forms are still complex
and requirements vary from municipality to municipality. Households also face
difficulties related to the management of impetants, property constraints or
encroachments on roads. These cumulative factors cause many project delays
and abandonments, and fuel a sense of inequality in the face of perceived
inconsistent and unreadable rules.
Administrative simplification is therefore a major lever to trigger a renovation
dynamic. It is based on three pillars: lighten and clarify permit applications,
harmonise and digitalise procedures, and strengthen support for citizens by
municipalities and professionals. The aim is to make the procedures
proportionate, legible and fast, while maintaining quality and durability
requirements for the work carried out.
quantified_objectives:
- 100% of planning permits introduced from 2028 will be
digitalized
- 80% of renovation permits introduced digitally by 2030.
- 25% reduction of supporting documents in energy renovation files by
2030.
budget:
- Development of a single digital platform: €€€
- Production of municipal maps: €€€€
- Revision and simplification of the forms: €€ €€
- Training and tools for municipalities: €€ €€
- Technical studies (alternative solutions, architectural quality guides):
€€ €€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, UVCW and communes, Order of Architects, professional
federations

measure_category: Establishing a structuring regulatory framework
measure_name: Measure 5 - Evolve the Housing audit tool
description:
The Housing of Buildings audit is an essential step in the renovation process:
it makes it possible to identify the most relevant works, plan their sequence and
direct owners to the appropriate support devices.
Complementary to the EPB+ certificate, whose main function is to assess
energy performance to allow comparison between goods and to provide
information on the overall scope of work to be considered, the audit stands out
as an operational and personalised advisory tool. Focused on the building and
its occupants, it takes into account the project for the development of the
property and must guide owners towards renovations that are coherent,
technically viable and compatible with the 2050 decarbonisation objectives.
The audits will also inform households about future regulatory requirements
(MEPS, fossil fuel exit) and identify mobilizable financing, to provide a clear
view of obligations and opportunities.
Today, the housing audit already makes it possible to clarify the stages of work,
but it must evolve to meet the challenges of the massive renovation of the built
stock. It is expected to become the obligatory passage of any project supported
by public aid, by proposing a clear roadmap towards deep renovation. It will
also have to be designed to be directly usable as part of the future building
passport. Finally, the audit must broaden its scope of analysis by integrating
health, safety, summer comfort and indoor air quality aspects, in order to
provide a comprehensive response to housing challenges. Its accessibility will
have to be guaranteed for vulnerable households, via appropriate public care.
quantified_objectives:
- Condition access to public financial support to a prior audit/expertise
report in 2027
- Double the annual number of housing audits carried out by 2030
compared to 2024 (40,000 audits/year)
- Ensuring audit accessibility for precarious households from 2027
budget:
- Subsidisation of audits for precarious households: €€€€
- Training and quality control of auditors: €€€€
stakeholders:
- SPW TLPE, Network of Accredited Housing Auditors, Points of Single
Contact, SWCS, FLW

measure_category: Establishing a structuring regulatory framework
measure_name: Measure 6 - Evolving EPB tools
description:
The EPB tools are a central pillar of Walloon energy policy. They assess,
compare and certify the energy performance of buildings, while guiding
renovation choices and monitoring progress. However, recent developments in
the European framework, the regional ambition to massively renovate and the
gradual introduction of renovation obligations require a modernisation of this
ecosystem.
This reform aims to adapt the EPB tools to the new requirements of the
European Directive 2024/1275, to simplify their use for citizens and
professionals, and to ensure the reliability of data. It is based on three
principles:
1. Simplification and consistency: Merge existing calculation methods
(new buildings, existing buildings, audits) into a unified and legible
method based on the harmonised European scale of energy classes
2. Professionalisation and monitoring: Strengthen the quality of the
work of certifiers and auditors, harmonise their profiles and training,
and make certificates issued by increased and systematic control more
reliable
3. Integration and transparency - Connecting EPB tools with digital

platforms for renovation and financial support, while providing citizens with
Description of the
enriched certificates (EPB+) and a digital building passport, the basis for
measurement
future renovation monitoring.
The EPB certificate will become a planning and decision support tool, capable
of automatically generating a renovation trajectory aligned with regional
obligations. The building passport, on the other hand, will offer a consolidated
and up-to-date view of the state of a dwelling, integrating administrative,
energy and technical information into a single interface.
Finally, the reform aims to reduce the gap between theoretical and actual
performance, strengthen public confidence in the certificates issued and ensure
a consistent use of these tools as a basis for regional renovation planning. It is
thus a decisive step towards achieving the objectives of carbon neutrality and
transparency set by Wallonia.
bonus schemes, becoming a pivotal document between the citizen and the
administration. In line with the new European requirements, the certificate will
also include information on indoor air quality, summer comfort and reduction
of operational GHG emissions, in order to become a more comprehensive
decision support tool.
Action 3: Deploy and test the building passport [2025– 2027]
The building passport will consist of a digital register accessible in a unique
and secure way to property owners (houses in the first instance) in Wallonia.
In its first version, it will provide access to and centralise all the administrative
and energy data of a property.
It will make it possible to deposit and organise documents relevant to the
management of a building, or to encode the work and maintenance carried out
and to attach the corresponding certificates. The aim is to enable the owner to
follow the building throughout its life cycle, improve traceability and facilitate
the transmission of information when changing properties. The passport will
better take into account the renovations already carried out. It will evolve
gradually to integrate other regional databases (financial support, permits),
other types of buildings (apartments, non-residential) and will be an essential
tool for the massification of renovations.
Action 4: Simplifying the landscape of EPB actors [2024–2029]
The current system is based on three distinct profiles (certificator, auditor, EPB
manager), resulting in perceived complexity for homeowners (residential / non-
residential) and burdensome for professionals. The action aims to simplify this
landscape, either by creating a single profile or by clarifying two separate
profiles (EPB certificate and designer). The reform will involve adapting the
accreditation conditions, harmonising training and improving fluidity between
roles. The aim is to reduce the number of people speaking to citizens, while
ensuring a high level of competence and increasing trust in the system. This
action is part of a logic of efficiency and rationalisation of the market.
Action 5: Reinforcing the training of professionals [2025–2035]
The credibility of the EPB system depends heavily on the quality of the
certifying staff's work. There is sometimes a lack of quality in the EPB
certificates issued: Encoding errors, diverging interpretations, etc. This action
foresees adapting the initial and continuous trainings to integrate more practice,
modules on frequent errors, case law and behaviour to be adopted in case of
difficulty with the applicant for the EPB certificate.
Action 6 : Strengthening quality control [2024-2040]
In the ev ent of non-compliance, the penalties will be tightened for
wrongful certifiers (temporary suspensions, administrative fines). Ultimately,
this professionalisation aims to improve the reproducibility of certificates,
reduce deviations and ensure the reliability of the tool, which is a key element
to ensure the consistency of renovation obligations. Consistent with European
requirements, control systems should include random sampling, on-site visits,
and aim for 90% compliance of certificates issued.
Action 7: Merging digital tools and ensuring their interoperability [2030-
2032]
Currently, two main software packages (PEB and PACE) coexist, with separate
databases for the new and existing ones. The TOTEM software is added to
them for environmental impact analysis. This leads to redundancies,
maintenance costs and loss of efficiency. The action plans to develop a single
tool based on the integrated methodology, which will bring together all the
functions necessary for the certification, audit and monitoring of buildings.
This tool will be designed to be interoperable with renovation platforms and
energy windows, in order to provide a central digital window for citizens and
professionals
Action 8: Enhancing communication with citizens and stakeholders [2026–
2028]
Better communication is essential to improve the acceptance and understanding
of the EPB certificate. The action foresees a broad campaign focusing on the
importance of certification and preparing the visit of the certifier (acceptable
evidence, necessary documents). In parallel, targeted communications will be
organised to certifyers to remind them of obligations, frequent errors and
penalties incurred in the event of fraud. This action also includes a
popularisation component to explain the logic of the renovation obligation and
its timing, in order to limit social resistance.
Action 9: Mandating post-work certification for publicly supported
renovations [2026–2028]
As provided for in the Comprehensive Renovation Support Scheme, any
publicly funded renovation will be followed by mandatory certification, in
order to verify the actual achievement of the energy targets. This obligation
will enhance the credibility of the system and allow accurate statistical
monitoring of actual gains. For precarious households, the cost of certification
may be borne in whole or in part by the public authorities, so as not to create a
financial barrier. The action will feed the regional databases with reliable data,
useful both for political steering and for monitoring European commitments.
Action 10: Reducing the gap between theoretical and actual consumption
[2026–2029]
The EPB certificate remains today based on a standardised theoretical
consumption, which often differs from the actual consumption observed, due
to the behaviour of the occupants, less comfort provided in an energy-efficient
housing, the use of technical systems or defects in the implementation of the
works. Although the purpose of the EPB certificate is not to estimate actual
consumption – but rather to be able to compare the energy status of dwellings
among themselves, this gap reduces the credibility of the tool with the general
public. The aim of the action is to provide for a reform of the EPB in order to
aim for a gradual reconciliation between theoretical and actual data, for
example by reviewing the meteorological data under consideration or the
default wall values.
quantified_objectives:
- Have a single EPB calculation method for residential: 2029
- Make the PEB+ certificate operational in 2026
- Reduce variations between certifiers by 50%
- In 2028, increase by 30% the number of checks on EPB certificates
carried out annually compared to 2025
- Ensure that 100% of the supported renovations are followed by post-
work certification in 2027
- 90% compliance of certificates checked in 2030
-
budget:
- Development of new tools: €€€
- Support for the financing of post-work residential EPB certificates
for the most vulnerable: €€ €€
- Reinforcement of control: €€€€
-
stakeholders:
- SPW TLPE, EPB actors (certificators, auditors, EPB managers), Energy
Window and Renovation Platforms

measure_category: Establishing a structuring regulatory framework
measure_name: Measure 7 - Introduce a renovation obligation for non-residential buildings
description:
The Walloon non-residential sector accounts for almost 30% of the total final
energy consumption of the building stock, with a significant diversity of uses:
offices, shops, healthcare facilities, educational buildings, hotels, cultural and
sports facilities. A significant part of this stock remains today highly energy-
intensive, often ageing, and dependent on fossil fuels.
To date, there is no comprehensive and harmonised tool to assess the energy
performance of the entire private non-residential stock. The residential has an
EPB certificate based on theoretical consumption, while public buildings are
certified based on actual consumption. This methodological heterogeneity
hampers the planning, monitoring and identification of priority buildings to be
renovated.
EU Directive 2024/1275 introduces for the first time minimum energy
performance standards (MEPS) for all non-residential buildings. Member
States must ensure that
•
Reduce the share of the most energy-intensive buildings by 16% by
2030;

•
Then by 26% by 2033;
Description of the
measure
This obligation entails a profound transformation of the Walloon framework:
definition of a common assessment methodology, establishment of a
comprehensive register of non-residential stock, adoption of progressive
regulatory thresholds and creation of a sectoral support system.
The measure is therefore structured around four axes:
1. Knowledge and classification of the fleet, via a unified assessment
tool and a centralised database
2. Regulatory framework, with performance thresholds, a system of
exemptions and gradual obligations
3. Technical and financial support, adapted to the specificities of the
non-residential sector (offices, SMEs, shops, etc.)
4. Transparency and monitoring, through mandatory posting and a
system of dissuasive sanctions.
This step-by-step approach will align Wallonia with the European calendar,
while creating the conditions for an orderly transition to a well-functioning,
sustainable and competitive non-residential stock.
Owners of buildings above these thresholds will have to undertake energy
renovations, with an obligation to present an up-to-date certificate or a
certificate of work carried out. This regulation will be accompanied by a
sectoral information campaign to anticipate deadlines.
Action 5: Provide for an exemption scheme for renovation obligations
[2026]
In accordance with the Directive, certain categories may be exempted
(classified heritage, places of worship, small buildings < 50 m2, temporary
use). The criteria must be public, precise and circumscribed. Compensation
mechanisms for large assemblies will be put in place to avoid any loss of
overall impact.
Action 6: Setting up a support system [2030–2040]
Specific technical and financial support for the non-residential sector will be
developed: one-stop shops, SME-friendly and self-employed aid, third-party
investment for supermarkets, cost-sharing mechanisms for commercial co-
ownership.
Action 7: Start control and display [2027–2033]
The buildings subject to the obligation will have to prove their compliance with
an updated EPB certificate. Visibly displaying performance remains applicable
for buildings receiving the public, increasing market pressure to incentivise
renovation. A system of regular notifications will be provided to remind
owners of deadlines.
Action 8: Implement monitoring and sanctions [2030–2033]
A register of non-compliant buildings will be created. Sanctions will include
fines proportional to the size and degree of exceedance, the limitation of certain
activities (e.g. via a ban on renting non-compliant spaces).
Action 9: Programme and inform on next steps [2026– 2050]
As early as 2026, the thresholds for 2040 will have to be defined and published
in order to provide visibility to investors and property managers. These
milestones will make it possible to plan a gradual trajectory towards a quasi-
zero energy non-residential fleet in 2040 and zero emissions in 2050. This
predictability will support deep renovations from the first intervention, in order
to avoid additional costs related to successive renovations.
quantified_objectives:
- Define and develop a performance assessment tool for non-
residential buildings operational in 2029
- 100% of non-residential stock must be certified by 2030 with
interim target of 75% by 2028Ensure compliance of the most energy-
intensive 16% by 2030 and 26% by 2033, as well as compliance with
the 2040 and 2050 targets.
-
budget:
- Methodological and IT developments: €€€€
- Development of the centralised database: €€€€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, Walloon Government

measure_category: Establishing a structuring regulatory framework
measure_name: Measure 8 - Introduce a progressive renovation obligation for the residential stock
description:
As the current pace of renovations is not sufficient, and the existing incentive
frameworks have not had the expected impact, the European Commission has
introduced, through the EPBD 2024/1275, the obligation for Member States to
set binding control points for the energy performance of buildings. Neighbouring
countries and other Belgian regions have already followed suit: in Flanders, since
2023, a new owner must achieve at least the D label within 5 years of a purchase;
in the Brussels Region, all dwellings must have an E label at least in 2033 and at
least C in 2045; in France and the Netherlands, the rental bans on dwellings G
and F have been applied gradually since 2024.
Wallonia is part of this movement as part of its 2023 Walloon Energy Climate
Plan, taking into account the reality of its built stock and the capacities of its
owners. The agreed strategic document provides for the establishment of a clear
schedule of obligations: with gradual bans on worst-performing labels when
changing ownership, and achieving the decarbonised A label on average by 2050.
The aim is to provide a predictable trajectory for households and the sector, while
avoiding technical lock-ins and social divides, through targeted accompanying
measures.
Two distinct mechanisms have been defined: one triggered by the transfer of the
property, the other concerning the possession of an existing dwelling.
Obligation related to change of ownership (Sale)
quantified_objectives:
- 100% of dwellings with a valid EPB certificate by 2030.
- Regulation of renovation obligations adopted in a legislative text in 2026
- At least 55% of efficiency gains from the 43% most energy-intensive
housing.
- 16% reduction in average primary energy consumption in 2030
compared to 2020
- 20% reduction in average primary energy consumption in 2035
compared to 2020
-
budget:
- Deployment of the EPB certificate: €€€€
- Communication and awareness-raising campaigns: €€€€
- Control and monitoring of requirements: €€€€
-
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, Walloon Government

measure_category: Establishing a structuring regulatory framework
measure_name: Measure 9 - Put in place a strategy to limit the rebound effect
description:
Energy renovation – be it the insulation of buildings or the replacement of
heating systems – is not an end in itself. Above all, it aims to reduce energy
consumption and, above all, greenhouse gas emissions on a sustainable basis.
However, several studies highlight the existence of a ‘rebound effect’: a share
of the expected savings is offset by an increased search for comfort (higher
indoor temperature, intensive use of equipment) or by inadequate use of
technical systems (heating, ventilation, home automation). In Wallonia, as
elsewhere, this effect can reach a significant percentage of the real gains
reflected in the theoretical gains, thus reducing the real effectiveness of public
policies and jeopardising the achievement of climate objectives. However, it is
essential to distinguish between situations. In some cases, in particular for
households in energy poverty, the increase in consumption observed
corresponds to a necessary improvement in basic comfort (temperature finally
adequate, proper ventilation). That ‘social rebound’ must be regarded as
positive and legitimate, since it contributes to improving health and quality of
life.
The EPBD 2024/1275 stresses the need to assess the actual performance after
renovation and to support users in the use of their buildings. It also highlights
the importance of monitoring and maintaining installations, as well as
transparency vis-à-vis occupants and authorities. Without an appropriate
strategy, there is a risk that citizens and investors will lose confidence in the
very interest of the renovation.
To limit the rebound effect, the Walloon strategy must rely on several
complementary levers: quality control of works, performance commitments of
professionals, systematic monitoring of real consumption, training and support
of users after works. It must also provide for structured feedback in order to
gradually improve the practices of the sector and better enhance the central
role of occupants in the success of the energy transition.
quantified_objectives:
- Explore the possibility of integrating post-renovationconsumption
monitoring for projects receiving public financial support as early as
2029.
- Train and support at least 10,000 renovating households by 2035 on
the optimal use of their homes.
budget:
- Adaptation of public financial support schemes for a network of post-
renovation coordinators/accompaniors: €€€€
- Development of monitoring tools (digital platforms, smart meters,
applications): €€ €€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, CEHD, auditors, SWL, AIS, training organisations and professional
federations, GRD and suppliers

measure_category: Decarbonising heat and strengthening local planning
measure_name: Measure 10 - Developing heat and cold plans at the local level
description:
The decarbonisation of heating and cooling is one of the major challenges of
the Walloon energy transition. It requires clear and coordinated technological
choices at neighbourhood level to ensure coherence between energy planning,
building renovation and spatial planning.
Depending on the density and urban morphology, different solutions can be
used: low or medium temperature heat networks, individual or collective heat
pumps, waste heat recovery (industry, data centres, treatment plants), shallow
geothermal, solar thermal, passive cooling solutions, etc.
Local heating and cooling plans (LCCPs), introduced by Article 25 of the
Energy Efficiency Directive (EU) 2023/1791, provide the methodological
framework for such planning. They make it possible to map energy needs and
potentials at municipal level , and locally translate the regional intervector
vision, ensuring the alignment of local renovation and development policies.
The measure aims to steer public and private investment towards no-regret
solutions, reduce systemic costs and ensure long-term optimisation of energy
infrastructure. The PLCFs will strengthen the capacity of municipalities to plan
and manage the energy transition in their territory, providing them with a
common methodology, standardised analysis tools, and structured regional
support for the preparation and implementation of the plans.
In addition, the PLCFs will serve as a lever for consistency and visibility:
•
They will facilitate the integration of energy priorities into planning
documents (schemas, BCPs, permits).
•
They will support proactive communication to households and
businesses, through neighbourhood maps, technology orientation
simulators and clear messages on bonds and available support.
By combining territorial energy planning, municipal tools and public
information, local heating and cooling plans will become a structuring
instrument of the Renovation Plan, ensuring coherent technical and economic
choices, increased collective efficiency and a fair transition at local level.
quantified_objectives:
- The regional framework and methodology for local heating and cooling
plans are published, and the Municipal Support Cell is operational by
2026.
budget:
- Methodology, tools and heat/cold cadastre: €€ €€
- Support cell and strengthening of municipalities
(assistance/training): €€ €€
- Studies and pre-development (feasibility, business models,
consultation): €€ €€
- Investment aid for first networks/pilots: €€€

measure_category: Decarbonising heat and strengthening local planning
measure_name: Measure 11 - Put in place a strategy for phasing out fossil fuels in the building sector by 2040
description:
Achieving climate neutrality by 2050 requires setting a course now: the typical
lifetime of a fossil boiler is approximately 15 years; any installation put into
operation after 2035 is likely to be still in operation in 2050, which contradicts
the carbon neutrality trajectory. Without a clear direction, technical and
economic locks are created (reverse investments, stranded assets, oversizing of
networks) and the transition is unnecessarily slowed down. This measure
therefore establishes a strategy of gradual and territorialized exit from fossil
fuels, with a reviewable phasing and gradual obligations.
Wallonia will thus organize a gradual and territorial shift from fossil fuels to
non-fossil solutions (individual/collective heat pumps, low/medium
temperature decarbonized heat networks, solar thermal, surface geothermal,
waste heat). The preparation of local heating and cooling plans (LCCPs) is the
prerequisite: these plans zone territories (priority heat networks, electrification,
transitional hybrids), sequence investments and provide thetechno-economic
basis for setting appropriate switching deadlines.
In heat network hotspots, success depends on the ability to reserve a minimum
heat demand upstream before the investment decision. This pre-contractual
commitment makes it possible to reassure operators and investors by securing
the revenue base and reducing the risk of underutilization of the facilities. Here,
public buildings play a key role in anchoring: their consumption profiles, their
long-term presence and their ability to commit to multi-annual horizons make
it possible to reach this start-up threshold quickly. Once the basis of the reliable
heating network, the extension to consumers of smaller sizes (private land,
condominiums, collective or individual housing, SMEs) becomes simpler,
faster and less risky.
Finally, the measure is linked to the proactive communication planned at local
level: maps by neighbourhood, technology orientation simulator, clear
messages on obligations and aid pathways. The aim is to align individual
decisions and collective investments with the zoning defined in the PLCFs, to
minimise the cost of the overall system and to avoid technical and economic
impasses.
quantified_objectives:
- Planning defined in 2030 on the basis of the complete exit vision of
fossil boilers by 2040 at the latest, and broken down by zones via
local heating and cooling plans.
-
budget:
- Legal framework, PLCF, governance & data amplification: €€ €€
- Aid for equipment targeted at vulnerable households: €€€€
- Decarbonisation of existing networks: €€€€
- Quality/training & proactive communication: €€ €€

measure_category: Decarbonising heat and strengthening local planning
measure_name: Measure 12 - Developing renewable alternatives in buildings
description:
Heating and cooling account for more than half of energy consumption in
Wallonia, almost 80% of which is still covered by gas and oil. This dependency
maintains a high economic and climate vulnerability. To reverse this trend,
Wallonia must support the rapid and massive substitution of fossil fuels by
local renewable sources. The measure aims to create a coherent and
competitive ecosystem of renewable solutions, while removing the obstacles
that slow down their diffusion: high investment costs, inadequate energy
taxation, lack of skills, and lack of visibility for households and public actors.
•
The measure thus pursues a twofold philosophy: Actively support
renewable alternatives, facilitating their uptake through tailored
support, training, planning and communication schemes
•
Removing the structural barriers that prevent their deployment, by
reforming the tax and regulatory frameworks, developing the skilled
workforce and ensuring the coherence of technological choices in the
territory.
The dynamic is based on an integrated and complementary approach: Local
heating and cooling plans (LCCPs) determine the most suitable areas for each
technology (heat grids, heat pumps, geothermal, solar thermal), while the fossil
energy phase-out strategy sets the associated deadlines and obligations.
This measure complements this framework by concretely supporting the
deployment of alternatives and by equipping actors to overcome economic,
technical and social barriers.
By integrating economic support, technical framework and citizen visibility,
this measure anchors the transition to renewable alternatives at the heart of the
Renovation Plan, guaranteeing an orderly, fair and credible exit from fossil
heating in Wallonia.
quantified_objectives:
- 2 TWh/year of useful heat will be produced in Wallonia by 2030 by
heat pumps
- 0.2 TWh/year of solar thermal heat will be generated by 2030
- 0.25 TWh/year of heat from deep geothermal and mining by 2030
budget:
- Renewable heat support and grid connection: €€€
- Social programmes and vulnerable household bonuses: €€€€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE; inter-municipal municipalities; GRD; heat/cold operators; CPAS
and housing companies; professional federations; certification and training
bodies.

measure_category: Accompanying the renovation
measure_name: Measure 13 - Supporting the practice of self-renovation
description:
Self-renovation refers to any renovation carried out by a developer – whether
owner, tenant or co-owner – with the support of an accompanying operator, the
work of which has an impact on the energy performance or health of the building.
Already well established in Wallonia, this practice allows many households to
improve their housing at a lower cost, while actively participating in the energy
transition. It addresses several structural challenges:
•
Shortage of skilled labour in the construction sector;
•
The increasing cost of renovation works,
•
The need to intervene quickly on an old built park;
•
The desire for autonomy of some citizens.
However, the lack of a legal, technical and insurance framework is currently
holding back its development. It creates uncertainties about the quality, safety and
traceability of the work carried out by individuals.
The measure therefore aims to structure, supervise and promote self-renovation
as a fully-fledged lever of the Renovation Plan. It will seek to reconcile three
imperatives:
•
Ensure the safety and quality of interventions;
•
To preserve freedom of action and accountability;
•
Ensure that the work carried out is compatible with regional energy
performance and sustainability objectives.
By structuring this framework, Wallonia will recognise self-renovation as a
complementary practice to professional renovation, contributing to the
acceleration and democratisation of the energy transition of the building stock.
quantified_objectives:
- At least 80% of self-renovation works achieve verified energy
performance (via audit or spot check) by 2035
- At least 500 accompanying persons shall be trained in self-renovation by
2030.
budget:
- Development of the legal framework, digital platform, guides and
training: €€€€
- Deployment of a network of accompanying persons and pilot schemes:
€€€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, PLRE, EmBuild, UWA, auditors (technical and administrative
support), associative network

measure_category: Accompanying the renovation
measure_name: Measure 14 - Promote the involvement of condominiums and syndics in renovation planning
description:
In Wallonia, condominiums account for an increasing share of the residential
stock and are concentrated in urban and peri-urban areas, where densification
led to the massive construction of apartment buildings in the 1960s-1990s.
These housing units have specific characteristics in relation to the issue of
energy renovation:
•
Complex governance: renovation decisions require qualified majorities
in the general assembly, which often leads to blockages.
•
Social and financial heterogeneity: within the same building there are
owner-occupiers, private landlords, precarious or elderly households,
which makes it difficult to agree on heavy investments.
•
Lack of planning: There are few condominiums that have a multi-
annual work plan that integrates the energy dimensions. Interventions
are often limited to emergencies (elevator, safety, watertightness).
•
Limited role of trustees: their mission focuses on day-to-day
management, whereas they should play a leading role in informing, activating
and supporting the renovation. The accumulated delay weighs on the energy
transition. Without specific action, a large part of this stock will not be able to
achieve the regional objectives of convergence towards an average A-label
residential stock by 2050. The renovation of condominiums is at the same time
a climate, social and heritage issue: it determines the future value of real estate,
the control of household burdens and the quality of life in urban centres.
The measure proposes to introduce a collective approach to energy
performance and planning. It is based on three structuring levers:
1. A collective energy certificate incorporating an average label
reflecting the performance of the building as a whole.
2. A roadmap for condominiums, combining global diagnosis and
multi-annual work plan.
3. An accompaniment taking into account the specified co-ownerships
The renovation of condominiums must become an element of the energy
renovation of the Walloon building, as well as the renovation of individual
housing and tertiary and public buildings.
quantified_objectives:
- 100% of condominiums have an EPB certificate by 2030 - 10 000
condominium roadmaps established by 2030
- 100 trustees and managers dealing with energy issues and
Project management assistance shall be provided by 2028.
- 10 000 condominiums are renovated by 2035
budget:
- Development of EPB tools and training: €€€
- Development of specific support, development of the AMO and
training of trustees: €€€
- Development of standardised tools: €€€€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, Federia, Municipalities and CPAS, SWCS/FLW, Federal
Administrations, Febelfin

measure_category: Helping all households plan and carry out their renovation
measure_name: Measure 15 - Reinforcing support for precarious and digitally fractured audiences
description:
Support for precarious households is a central issue of the Renovation Plan.
While the achievement of climate objectives requires a massive renovation of
the residential stock, a significant part of the population remains remote from
information, hampered by administrative or digital barriers, and excluded from
support schemes.
These households also suffer the most from energy poverty: unpaid bills,
thermal discomfort, pathologies linked to unhealthy housing, and a sense of
exclusion in the face of procedures deemed complex or inaccessible.
The measure aims to make renovation truly accessible to all, by acting on four
complementary levers:
1. Better targeting of efforts through a fine-grained mapping of
vulnerabilities, making it possible to adapt approaches according to
territories, household profiles and housing types.
2. Reinforce communication and trust through clear and concrete
messages, focusing on health, comfort and savings, disseminated under
a specific visual identity and relayed by a network of local actors
(CPAS, associations, energy counters, etc.) and mobile hotlines.
3. Turning awareness into a complete journey: each contact will give
rise to a proactive follow-up, with quick appointments and guaranteed
deadlines. The steps will be simplified and supported by a ‘digital
splint’, integrated with existing services, to accompany unfamiliar
households step by step with online tools.
4. Provide long-term support through the appointment of a trusted
contact person, guaranteeing continuity of monitoring and
coordination with technical and social operators.
By articulating proximity, clarity, simplicity and continuity, this measure aims
to better target, inform and support precarious households, while removing the
financial, administrative and digital barriers that currently hinder their access
to energy renovation.
quantified_objectives:
- 80% of municipalities have an energy-precarity vulnerability map
and use it to adapt their local actions by 2030.
- 10 000 precarious households will have benefited from a pathway
complete renovation between 2026 and 2030.
-
budget:
- Tools and monitoring (vulnerability mapping, assessment and
continuous improvement): €€ €€
- Support and follow-up of households (referrers, site and post-work
coaching, simplification of procedures, digital splint, permanences
and ambassadors): €€€
- Bridge subsidy for access to finance: €€€€
- Home health aid (small pre-renovation interventions): -
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, SPW social action, AVIQ, communes, CPAS, intercommunales,
one-stop-shop and other support operators

measure_category: Helping all households plan and carry out their renovation
measure_name: Measure 16 - Supporting collective renovation
description:
Accompanied group renovation is a key lever to accelerate the energy transition
of the Walloon building stock. Faced with a still insufficient pace of renovation,
this collective approach makes it possible to scale up interventions, reduce costs
through economies of scale, and increase ownership by simplifying their
journey. It is based on level 3 integrated support, i.e. comprehensive support
ranging from the study of the project to the reception of the works, the search
for funding and the coordination of stakeholders.
Beyond the individual scale, group renovation promotes coherent planning of
public and private investments, integrating energy, climate and social
challenges at neighbourhood level. In Wallonia, the Reno+ experience has
demonstrated the full potential of this approach, but also the need for a
structured framework, articulating regional steering and local initiatives.
The measure provides for:
1. The creation of a regional support platform, integrated into the
Walloon Housing Agency, involving public and private actors. The
platform will be tasked with equipping, supporting and coordinating
local pooled renovation initiatives, building on the experience gained
by Reno+.
2. Deployment of “renovationtrains”,i.e. worksites carried out
simultaneously on similar dwellings, allowing for an industrialised,
replicable and faster approach.
3. District-based programming, integrating energy renovation, local
energy infrastructure (heat networks, distribution, storage) and climate
adaptation developments.
Thus, accompanied group renovation could become an operational pillar of the
Renovation Plan, combining economic efficiency, simplicity for households
and collective impact at territorial level.
quantified_objectives:
- 2,000 housing units renovated collectively between 2026 and 2030.
- At least 10 refurbishment trains deployed by 2029.
- 25% of municipalities with a local pooled renovation strategy
supported by the Regional Support Platform by 2030.
budget: - Regional support platform and common tools: €€€€
stakeholders:
SPW – Agence wallonne de l’Habitation, communes et intercommunales, SWL
and AIS (public housing), RENO+ (Buildwise and Embuild), professional
federations and construction companies, social and associative stakeholders.

measure_category: Helping all households plan and carry out their renovation
measure_name: Measure 17 - Develop one-stop-shops for renovation support
description:
Support is now dispersed between several actors and channels, which
limits the effectiveness of renovation pathways. Households and
tertiary structures must navigate between financial support,
administrative requirements and complex technical choices, often
without a clear vision. This fragmentation can lead to discouragement
of renovation projects and low uptake of existing schemes.
Support can offer three levels of service:
•
Type 1: information and guidance: quick and easy access
to information on relevant aid, obligations and interlocutors.
•
Type 2: personalised support for each project: technical,
administrative and financial advice adapted to the project,
with follow-up from start to finish.
•
Type 3: Delegation of the contracting authority: Full
support of the renovation project by a trusted third party.
The measure aims to gradually structure support around a one-stop
shop able to provide the first two types of support in the short term.
quantified_objectives:
- Harmonisation of advisory tools and audience segmentation
for differentiated cost coverage by October 2028.
- Territorial coverage: a one-stop shop for at least 80,000
inhabitants or accessible in less than 90 minutes in 2026.
- 100% of owners of non-residential buildings
low energy performance invited to a one-stop shop by 2030.
- An integrated renovation service offering the 2 levels of
support throughout the operational territory in 2028
- An integrated renovation service offering the 3 levels of
support throughout the operational territory in 2040
budget:
- Deployment and operation of physical and digital counters:
€€€
- Harmonisation of tools and development of the digital
platform: €€
- Training and certification of advisers: €€ €€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, housing agency, energy counters, renovation platforms,
Ordre des Architectes, professional federations

measure_category: Helping all households plan and carry out their renovation
measure_name: Measure 18 - Supporting the renovation of rental housing
description:
The Walloon rental park concentrates a large part of the most energy-intensive
housing. These dwellings weigh heavily on tenant households. The main
obstacle to the activation of energy renovation lies in the well-known split
incentive mechanism: the owners bear the cost of the works while the energy
savings benefit the tenants. This misalignment discourages many landlords,
especially small owners, from engaging in deep renovations. Conversely, when
works are carried out, some tenants are faced with a sudden increase in rents,
or even a ‘renovation’, that is to say, a disguised eviction after renovation.
The renovation of the rental fleet must therefore meet a twofold challenge:
•
social, ensuring access to decent, safe and affordable housing, without
a net increase in housing costs (rent + energy);
•
Energy-efficient, by phasing out energy sieves and aligning the rental
fleet with the European energy performance targets for 2030 and 2035,
namely a reduction in primary energy consumption in residential
buildings of at least:
-16% between 2020 and 2030.
o
-20 to -22 % by 2035.
o
Achieving this transformation requires combining several levers: a clear
framework for renovated rents, attractive incentives for landlords, enhanced
support by actors on the ground such as AIS, and transparent monitoring
mechanisms. The aim is to create a balanced framework that promotes the
massive renovation of the rental stock while protecting tenants, especially the
most vulnerable.
quantified_objectives:
- Reduce rental housing classified as F or G by 50% by 2030.
- Reach 20,000 rental units accompanied via AIS or one-stop shops by
2033.
budget:
- Direct aid to lessors coupled with conditions on rents: €€€
- Reinforcement of the AIS and financing of the temporary relocation
fund: €€€
- Monitoring arrangements, pilot projects and targeted communication:
€€€€
-
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, IWEPS/CEHD, Walloon Government, municipalities and CPAS,
federations, SNPC, AWCCLP

measure_category: Financing renovation
measure_name: Measure 19 - Developing innovative financing mechanisms
description:
Financing energy renovation is now the main barrier to action and scale-up in
Wallonia. More than half of the owners do not have the resources to finance
significant work. Existing mechanisms are not sufficient to mobilise all
audiences. The lower middle classes, senior citizens, small landlords or
condominiums face structural obstacles: inadequate borrowing conditions, lack
of collateral, administrative complexity or lack of readability of the
arrangements.
Renovation dynamics therefore remain constrained by misalignment between
investment cost, profitability and financing and debt capacity. The
conventional financing instruments envisaged (premiums combined with the
renopack) tend to concentrate the most substantial support on the most
precarious households, other tools would be provided to the more solvent
households in order to remove the obstacles to a major renovation.
This measure aims to build a coherent and complementary portfolio of
innovative financing tools, able to respond to the diversity of situations and
attract public and private capital in renovation. It is based on three pillars:
1. A regional innovative financing fund responsible for piloting,
pooling and guaranteeing the various instruments;
2. Financial products adapted to each profile: deferred repayment
loans, third-party investment, asset-backed loans, renovation leases,
land transfers;
3. Strategic partnerships mobilizing public actors (SPW, SWCS, FLW,
WE) and the banking sector, around common rules of quality,
transparency and performance.
The challenge is twofold: ensuring equitable access to finance while ensuring
the fiscal sustainability of regional policy. By promoting the re-use of capital
and maximising leverage, these mechanisms are expected to significantly
increase the volume of private investment in renovation by 2040. The actions
detailed below reflect this strategy: rapid testing of priority instruments,
structuring of a clear legal framework, and gradual generalisation throughout
Wallonia.
quantified_objectives:
- Implement at least three innovative financing instruments by 2030.
- Mobilise €200 million of private capital through guarantees and co-
financing by 2030.
- Analyse the possibility of launching pilot projects for the partial
transfer of bare-property
budget:
- Legal framework, governance and steering tools: €€€€
- Direct financing tools for households and operators: €€€
- Guarantee and de-risking instruments: €€€€
stakeholders:
- SPW - Wallonia Entreprendre - SWCS - FLW -SFPIM -
Intercommunales - AIS - banks - Social impact cooperatives.

measure_category: Financing renovation
measure_name: Measure 20 - Reforming financial support for renovation
description:
The policy of financial support for energy renovation in Wallonia has been built
over time by stacking successive schemes (premiums, Rénopack, MEBAR,
etc.) each responding to specific needs, sometimes with a lack of overall
coherence. This system, which is now fragmented, may lack legibility,
regulatory instability and limited budgetary efficiency. The report developed
by the CEHD and the HCS in June 2025 draws a precise conclusion: too long
payment deadlines, modified income scales without predictability, lack of pre-
financing for modest households, and inadequate socio-economic targeting.
These weaknesses hinder the massification of renovation and accentuate
inequalities between households that are or are not able to invest.
In a context where renovation becomes a European regulatory obligation and a
pillar of the Walloon Energy-Climate Plan, the reform of support aims to
rebuild a global, stable and socially just regime. It will pursue three objectives:
1. Simplify devices and make them predictable over several years
2. Adapting support to the real financing capacity of households
3. Maximising leverage by combining public support and private capital.
This reform of financial support is part of the overall scheme integrating the
reform of financial support, the introduction of renovation bonds and the
upgrading of household support. The reform orientation aims at a single and
stable premium-loan scheme, meaning the merging of premium schemes into a
reinforced lending scheme with pre-financing and a social safety net premium
for households with low financing capacity. The reform is based on a portfolio
philosophy of tools modulated according to solvency:
- Low-income households will benefit from a social net premium
coupled with a zero-interest loan and pre-financing
- Intermediate households will have access to subsidised loans
- Higher revenues will be oriented towards market solutions, while
maintaining access to support and guarantee funds.
The schemes will be merged, streamlined and steered by regulations ensuring
consistency between taxation, innovative financing and budget monitoring.
The package will form the basis for the future comprehensive renovation
support scheme, ensuring a balance between social justice, climate impact and
sustainability of public finances.
quantified_objectives:
- Reunite 100% of premium and loan schemes by 2027.
- Focus on the most energy-intensive dwellings (F–G) by 2027.
budget:
- Loan scheme 0%-Single premium/Loans at reduced rates: €€€
- Modulated loan portfolio: €€€
- Revised MEBAR: €€
- Co-ownership guarantee: €€
- Piloting, evaluation, observatory: €
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, SWCS, FLW, CPAS,

measure_category: Mobilising public and private exemplarity
measure_name: Measure 21 - Leading by example through ambitious renovation of public buildings
description:
The public park is a central lever to drive the renovation dynamic. With
thousands of buildings managed by the public authorities in Wallonia
(administrations, schools, sports infrastructure, rest homes, cultural centres,
etc.), their energy consumption and outdated state weigh heavily on public
finances and regional greenhouse gas emissions.
Beyond the direct impact on energy consumption, the renovation of public
buildings plays an essential role as an example: Local and regional authorities
are expected to lead the way, experiment with innovative solutions and give
confidence to households and businesses alike. Each renovated school, each
communal house transformed into an efficient and comfortable building
becomes a showcase of the energy transition and a positive signal sent to
society.
The European Union has strengthened this exemplary role. The Energy
Efficiency Directive2023/1791 requires the annual renovation of at least 3% of
the useful floor area of public buildings owned by public authorities, aiming at
near-zero energy consumption and zero emissions by 2040. At the same time,
the EED Directive revised in 2023 sets an annual reduction obligation of 1.9%
of final energy consumption in the public sector, as well as an obligation to
carry out renovation passports for 3% of the area. These obligations require
greater mobilization, both financially and in terms of governance and training
of public managers. Moreover, public buildings are not required to comply with
the renovation obligations laid down for the residential and non-residential
sectors.
In Wallonia, several instruments are available to the public authorities:
UREBA grants, the Renowatt mechanism, the POLLEC programme and tool,
the TOTEM tool for life cycle analysis, and energy monitoring initiatives. But
these tools remain too dispersed and suffer from a lack of coherence and
visibility, especially for small municipalities. This measure therefore aims to
consolidate and amplify existing tools, ensure compliance with European
obligations, and put the renovation of public buildings on a clear and legible
path.
quantified_objectives:
- Reduce final energy consumption in the public sector by 1.9% per
year (2026–2040).
- Achieve 100% QZEB non-residential public park by 2040.
budget:
- Design, interoperability and digitalisation: €€ - Financing of
renovations: €€€€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, Walloon Government, public bodies

measure_category: Mobilising public and private exemplarity
measure_name: Measure 22 - Renovating public housing in an exemplary manner
description:
The public housing stock in Wallonia is a pillar of housing policy and the
energy transition. Composed of around 110 000 units, it is mainly home to
vulnerable households with precarious or modest incomes. More than half of
these homes have not yet undergone deep renovation. This leads to
disproportionate heating loads, health and comfort problems, and exacerbates
social inequalities.
The exemplary renovation of this park is both a social and climate requirement.
It must enable tenants to benefit from decent, economical and healthy housing,
while respecting European and regional commitments to reduce greenhouse
gas emissions. The Walloon renovation strategy sets a clear and ambitious
objective: Achieving the decarbonised EPB A label on average by 2040, at a
sustained rate of 5,000 renovated dwellings per year.
This can only succeed by mobilising strong governance, innovative financing
mechanisms, the establishment of the housing agency and one-stop-shops, and
enhanced social support. It also implies taking into account the energy quality
of housing in the calculation of social rent. Finally, this renovation must play
an exemplary role: Each renovated social housing complex must be a showcase
of the Walloon energy transition and inspire the renovation of the private park.
quantified_objectives:
- Achieve an annual rate of 5,000 renovations on average by 2027.
- Renovate 25,000 unrenovated public housing units by 2030.
- Maintain a level of accessible rents for 100% of households housed
after renovation – taking into account hot rent.
budget:
- Strengthening local capacity (SLSP/AIS training, monitoring and
follow-up by CEHD/IWEPS): €
- Regional direct grants to co-finance the work of SLSPs and AISs and
to finance support for tenants: €€
- Establishment of renovation funds: €€€
- Heavy renovation works: €€€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, SWL/CEHD, AIS, communes and intercommunales, IWEPS

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 23 - Integrating climate resilience criteria into renovations
description:
Climate change exposes Walloon buildings to increasing risks: heat waves,
runoff, droughts, extreme winds. These hazards directly affect the comfort,
durability and health of occupants, but are still insufficiently integrated into
renovation projects.
This measure aims to structure the gradual integration of climate resilience
throughout the renovation process, from stakeholder awareness to post-work
monitoring, adapting existing tools, benchmarks and incentives. It has a
twofold objective:
•
Strengthen the capacity of the renovated building to withstand future
climatic hazards;
•
To equip professionals and project leaders to integrate these
dimensions into the design, diagnosis and execution of construction
sites.
To achieve this, Wallonia will develop diagnostic devices, regulatory
requirements, including those of the EPB, financial support mechanisms and
design practices in public and private procurement.
The inclusion of resilience criteria in audits, bonuses, specifications and
technical guides will make the implementation of solutions adapted to the local
context more readable and accessible: high-performance insulation, sun
protection, greening, rainwater management, choice of sustainable materials.
By structuring public action around climate criteria differentiated according to
exhibition areas and building typologies, the measure aims to increase the
effectiveness and relevance of renovation policies. It creates the conditions for
a change of scale, building on existing tools (notably the GRO), exemplary
pilot projects and an effort to train and disseminate good practices.
quantified_objectives:
- 100% of audits issued from 2030 onwards include a component "
Climate Vulnerability
- 100% of subsidised regional public renovation contracts
use the GRO tool with climate resilience criteria by 2030.
- ≥ 25 % of subsidised renovations in exposed areas include
climate adaptation solutions by 2030.
- 80% of renovation support structures trained
climate resilience challenges by 2029.
- Implementation of at least 10 public pilot projects integrating
adaptation measures between 2026 and 2032.
budget:
Budget:
•
Regulatory: €€ €€
•
Training and awareness-raising: €€ €€
•
Financial incentive: €€€ (a few M€/year)
•
Exemplary public buildings – pilot renovation: €€€
stakeholders: SPW TLPE, AWAC, Municipalities, Wallonia Housing Fund, Energy Banks

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 24 - Reducing the carbon footprint of buildings
description:
Until now, the climate performance of buildings has been assessed mainly
through their energy consumption in the use phase (heating, ventilation,
lighting). However, this approach covers only part of the problem: greenhouse
gas emissions associated with the entire life cycle of the building - extraction
of raw materials, production of materials, transport, construction, maintenance,
demolition and waste treatment - now account for a significant share and
increasing from balance sheet carbon
global.
As buildings become more energy efficient, the ‘grey’ emissions of materials
(concrete, steel, glass, insulators) become more and more important in the total
footprint.
Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the energy performance of buildings introduces
for the first time an obligation to assess and display the life cycle carbon
footprint (LCA) of new buildings. This requirement will apply from 2028 for
buildings over 1000 m2 and from 2030 for all new buildings. Each Member
State will also have to publish, by 2027, a national roadmap setting degressive
limit values per building type. This development marks a paradigm shift:
carbon performance is no longer limited to operational energy efficiency, but
extends to all stages of the life cycle of the building.
Wallonia already has a recognised operational tool that meets these
requirements: TOTEM, developed jointly with Flanders and Brussels. The
measure therefore aims to institutionalise TOTEM as an official Global
Warming Potential Assessment (GWP) tool for new buildings and ultimately
for deep renovations. Its ambition is to ensure a faithful, pragmatic and
progressive transposition of European requirements, while mobilising existing
tools, developing calculation and monitoring capacity and supporting
stakeholders in the sector (architects, design offices, developers, public
authorities).
Beyond the regulatory obligation, this measure is part of a logic of circular
economy and material sobriety. It supports the transition to low-carbon
construction in Wallonia. This integrated approach will allow the Region to
align its renovation and construction policies with the European climate
neutrality objectives, while stimulating innovation and the upskilling of the
sector. Ultimately, carbon assessment will become a tool to support public and
private decision-making, ensuring consistency between energy performance,
material sustainability and overall environmental impact.
quantified_objectives:
- Use TOTEM for 100% of new public buildings over 1,000 m2 from
2028, then for all new buildings from 2030
- Publish at the end of 2026 a regional roadmap setting limit values by
typology
- Train professionals to use TOTEM by 2030.
- Reduce by 40% the global warming impact on the entire life cycle of
new and renovated buildings by 2040 compared to 2020.
budget:
- Updating and interoperability of TOTEM with PEB: €€€€
- Training and certification of professionals: €€€€
stakeholders:
- SPW TLPE, SPW Sustainable Development, VITO, Circular Wallonia,
other regions

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 25 - Deploy a strategy to train, attract and retain the workforce
description:
The success of the Walloon energy renovation plan for buildings is based on a
human factor above all: availability, competence and retention of the
workforce. Today, the Region faces an estimated structural deficit of between
20 000 and 25 000 full-time equivalents by 2030 if the current pace of training
continues.
Tensions affect the entire value chain: from field occupations (insulators,
roofers, heaters, carpenters) to management occupations (architects, auditors,
project management assistants). Recruitment time is significantly longer in
construction occupations, sometimes more than several months for technical
functions, and 9 out of 10 construction companies have fewer than 10 workers,
limiting their ability to grow.
The shortage is twofold: quantitative, with a lack of effective labour, and
qualitative, with a lack of skills on bio-based materials, efficient renovation,
cross-industrycoordination and the use of digital tools. Tensions in the labour
market also have a territorial dimension: the Luxembourg and Namur basins
suffer from a lack of training provision, while Hainaut concentrates a high
demand for skilled labour in the HVAC and insulation trades.
Faced with this observation, the strategy aims to recruit, train and retain a new
generation of renovation workers, while accompanying the transformation of
the sector.
It is based on four complementary levers:
1. Plan: The Regional Observatory for Trades and Renovation
(dedicated sheet) will provide the statistical and prospective basis for
calibrating needs by basin and occupation.
2. Train: modernise curricula and develop the requalification of
declining professions.
3. Attract and include: make jobs more visible, attractive and
accessible to all audiences (young people, women, retraining workers).
4. Retain and structure: building business capacity, improving
productivity and establishing a sustainable quality framework.
quantified_objectives:
- Train or reskill 10 000 people in renovation occupations by 2030.
- Achieve 40% sustainability orientation in technical and vocational
education by 2030.
- Increase the share of women in renovation occupations to 15% by
2030.
-
budget:
- Initial, continuing and requalification training (centres, instructors,
technical modules): €€€
- Educational infrastructure (platforms, school sites,
sustainable campuses): €€€
- Business support, mentoring, hiring support, communication: €€

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 26 - Developing the circular economy in the construction sector
description:
Construction and renovation still generate high resource consumption and
waste generation today, even though Wallonia has a high potential for re-use
and recovery of materials. This measure aims to gradually integrate circular
economy principles into construction and renovation projects, mobilising both
existing regulatory, financial and technical tools and developing new levers
adapted to the needs of the sector.
It responds to a twofold challenge: reduce the environmental impact of
buildings by maximising the reuse of resources, and support professionals
towards more sustainable and innovative practices. Concretely, this means
adapting the specifications and urban planning rules to promote reversible and
removable design, making certain financial aid conditional on the use of
sustainable materials or re-use, and supporting demonstration projects to test
and disseminate circular solutions.
Implementation will also require stronger support for local and regional
players: training of technical services, provision of guides and repositories,
pooling of diagnostic tools, creation of standard frameworks for public
procurement. Feedback from pilot projects will be used to accelerate the uptake
of these practices, both in public and private construction sites, and to make
circularity operational across the territory.
quantified_objectives:
- 100% of the public projects concerned carry out a diagnosis
Re-use/maintenance before permit by 2029.
- 1000 trained/certified “circular skills” professionals
by 2030.
- Integrated regional window operational in 2027;
- ≥ 150 demonstrator projects supported 2026–2030.
budget:

- Training (training, “circularity” coaches): €€€€
- Economic (calls for projects, grants): €€€
- Development/strengthening of tools (guides, fiches, standard
templates): €€€€
- Communication/Awareness-raising (one-stop-shop, campaigns,
information dissemination): €€€€

- Training: SPW Economy, FOREM
- Pilot projects: ACER, Greenwin

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 27 - Promoting modular, industrialised and innovative solutions
description:
The challenge of massive renovation in Wallonia requires going beyond the
traditional model of craft construction, which is often expensive, long and
complex for both households and public authorities. Modular, industrialised
and innovative solutions – inspired by approaches such as EnergieSprong or
GigaRegioFactory – make it possible to speed up intervention rates, standardise
quality, reduce unit costs and minimise nuisance to occupants. They consist of
designing deep renovations as reproducible products (prefabricated insulation
panels, integrated technical modules, industrialised solar roofs, plug-and-play
facades, etc.) rather than as one-off projects.
This approach is fully in line with the spirit of the EPBD 2024/1275, which
encourages the massification of renovations by industrialised, reproducible and
compatible with European standards (ZEB – Zero Emission Buildings). It is
also a strategic lever for the Walloon economic fabric: By stimulating the
creation of prefabrication production sites, dedicated supply chains and public-
private partnerships, Wallonia can position itself at the forefront of low-carbon
renovation in Europe.
Finally, the use of industrialised solutions promotes the upskilling of the
workforce and the creation of new skilled jobs in the construction sector. It also
paves the way for the integration of digital technologies (BIM, digital twins,
performance monitoring) and innovative approaches (circular renovation, low-
carbon materials, bio-based solutions). This measure is designed in conjunction
with collective renovation, which is the preferred terrain for deploying these
processes on a large scale.
With regard to sustainable mobility, buildings are preferred parking places and
therefore strategic anchor points for developing sustainable mobility: charging
stations for electric vehicles, pre-equipment for their future deployment, secure
bicycle parking adapted to new uses (cargo bikes, electric bikes, adapted bikes).
Directive EPBD 2024/1275 now imposes clear obligations to accelerate the
development of recharging and bicycle parking infrastructure in new,
renovated and existing buildings, in line with the Alternative Fuels
Infrastructure Deployment Regulation. Wallonia must transpose these
obligations by ensuring that they are applied in a proportionate and operational
manner, directly linked to the energy renovation of buildings. The
implementation of energy works is a good time to design the integration of
sustainable mobility.
quantified_objectives:
- 1,000 homes renovated via industrialized solutions and
modular by 2035.
- 20% reduction in average unit costs of deep renovations by 2035
thanks to industrialised and modular solutions.
- 80% of the evaluated pilot projects compatible with the standard
ZEB by 2030.
- 100% of new public terminals and 80% of private terminals
accessible installed between 2027 and 2030 comply with accessibility
criteria.
- Publication of the guide to minimum technical requirements for
mobility in 2028
budget:
- Support for industrialised and innovative production sites: €€€
- Seed fund for innovative projects: €€€
- Pilot programmes such as EnergieSprong: €€
- Digital tools: €
- Training and re-skilling of workers: €€
stakeholders:
SPW TLPE, communes and intercommunales, SWL, AIS, building federations
(Embuild, Buildwise), Wallonia Entreprendre, industrial clusters (GreenWin,
EcoConstruction), innovation players (research centres, universities), pilot
companies in the construction and prefabrication sector.

measure_category: Developing a quality and resilient offer
measure_name: Measure 28 - Securing the supply of materials via local and circular sectors
description:
The transition of the construction sector to a low carbon and circular model
depends directly on the availability, quality and traceability of the materials
used. However, Wallonia remains highly dependent on imports of conventional
materials (concrete, steel, fossil insulators) and still has fragmented local value
chains for bio-based materials. This dependency increases the sector’s
vulnerability to supply pressures, price volatility and geopolitical risks, while
limiting regional economic spillovers.
The measure aims to structure, diversify and secure the supply of sustainable
materials through the development of local and resilient supply chains, capable
of fuelling the growing demand resulting from renovation and sustainable
construction policies. It complements the circular economy measure: where the
latter recovers existing resources and limits waste, this measure acts upstream,
on the production and processing of materials.
Public action will focus on four main areas:
1. Know and map available resources, regional deposits and missing
links in value chains. Sectoral analyses will identify priority sectors
and assess their needs in terms of infrastructure, training and logistics.
2. Support industrial structuring through targeted financial
mechanisms and calls for projects, promoting investment in the
production, processing and certification of sustainable materials.
3. Ensure quality and trust by harmonising standards, establishing a
certification framework, and disseminating technical benchmarks.
4. Creating a strong and visible market by stimulating local demand:
inclusion of sustainability and traceability criteria in public
procurement, promotion of exemplary projects, sectoral
communication campaigns and training of prescribers.
In the long term, the measure must enable the creation of an integrated Walloon
industrial ecosystem capable of providing competitive, sustainable and
certified materials, reducing dependence on imports and strengthening
Wallonia’s economic and climate sovereignty. It will also contribute to the
creation of non-relocatable jobs, support to rural territories and resilience of
supply chains in a changing European context.
quantified_objectives:
- 10 sectoral studies to identify priority sectors
will be achieved by 2030
- Increase the annual production of circular materials in Wallonia by
30% by 2030.
- Organise 10 communication campaigns and sector events by 2030
budget:

- Integration of public procurement: €€€€
- Public exemplary projects: €€€
- Communication campaigns: €€€€
- Quality framework and standardisation: €€€€

Finances – Public procurement; Greenwin; Buildwise; professional
federations; municipalities.
